coredata.dc:title: Polymer-Enhanced glucose biosensors with UV sensitivity: advancements in photoelectrochemical energy systems using response surface methodology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ashakirin Binti Mohd Nashruddin, Siti Nur
Mohamed Salleh, Faridah Hani
Sulaiman, Nur Fatin
Razipwee, Mohd Farhanulhakim M.
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical biosensor detection based on enzymes for monitoring biochemical substances remains appealing. This study investigates an innovative glucose biosensor with enhanced ultraviolet (UV) light sensitivity using a composite nanomaterial of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS), MXene (Ti3C2), and graphene quantum dots (GQD) modified onto a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE). The immobilization of glucose oxidase (GOx) on this PEDOT/MXene/GQD-modified SPCE exhibited superior electrochemical behavior, characterized through FTIR, XPS, SEM, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The biosensor showed a linear voltammetric response within a glucose concentration range of 0–500 μM, with a low detection limit and consistent performance under ambient, dark, and UV light conditions, indicating robustness in stability and selectivity. Response surface methodology (RSM) was applied to optimize operational parameters, revealing through ANOVA that current density (F-value = 22.44) was the most critical factor. Glucose was determined with a sensitivity of 5.41 μAμM−1 cm−2 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 44.60 μM. These UV light photo-sensing biosensors enhance glucose detection and demonstrate potential for applications in biomedical diagnostics energy conversion efficiency and system monitoring, underscoring their role in advancing sustainable electrochemical energy systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose
Sustainable energy
Electrochemistry
PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of MXene (A) and FESEM of GQD (B).

Cyclic voltammetry scan rate (A) with correlation regression line (B) of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx. The effect of pH (C) and glucose oxidase (100 U/mL) layers (D) on the electrode surface (E) Interference with 50 µM glucose.

DPV of glucose concentration effect in conditions of ambient (A), dark (B), and UV-light (C) at different concentrations of glucose. The performance of the current density at 500 µM glucose concentration. (D).

Diagnostic plots of A. The normal probability plot of residuals, B. Residuals versus run number, C. Box-Cox plot for power transform, and D. Actual data versus predicted data for the current density of composite polymers.
3D and 2D contour plots of the current density for composite polymers of A. pH and scan rate, B. pH and enzyme layers, and C scan rate and enzyme layers.
Comparison of the proposed method with previously published assays for enzymatic glucose detection.

head.ce:title: Polymer-Enhanced glucose biosensors with UV sensitivity: advancements in photoelectrochemical energy systems using response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Informatics and Computing in Energy (IICE), Department of Computing, College of Computing & Informatics, University of Tenaga Nasional, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Informatics and Computing in Energy (IICE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Informatics and Computing in Energy (IICE), Department of Computing, College of Computing & Informatics, University of Tenaga Nasional, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics (IMEN), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Bangi 43600 Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics (IMEN), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Bangi 43600 Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical biosensor detection based on enzymes for monitoring biochemical substances remains appealing. This study investigates an innovative glucose biosensor with enhanced ultraviolet (UV) light sensitivity using a composite nanomaterial of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS), MXene (Ti3C2), and graphene quantum dots (GQD) modified onto a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE). The immobilization of glucose oxidase (GOx) on this PEDOT/MXene/GQD-modified SPCE exhibited superior electrochemical behavior, characterized through FTIR, XPS, SEM, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The biosensor showed a linear voltammetric response within a glucose concentration range of 0–500 μM, with a low detection limit and consistent performance under ambient, dark, and UV light conditions, indicating robustness in stability and selectivity. Response surface methodology (RSM) was applied to optimize operational parameters, revealing through ANOVA that current density (F-value = 22.44) was the most critical factor. Glucose was determined with a sensitivity of 5.41 μAμM−1 cm−2 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 44.60 μM. These UV light photo-sensing biosensors enhance glucose detection and demonstrate potential for applications in biomedical diagnostics energy conversion efficiency and system monitoring, underscoring their role in advancing sustainable electrochemical energy systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the demand for reliable and accurate glucose biosensors is ever-increasing [1], especially with the rising prevalence of diabetes and the need for monitoring tools [2]. In this context, the development of UV-resistant glucose biosensors is crucial for ensuring the stability and accuracy of glucose measurements, particularly in environments where exposure to UV light is unavoidable [3]. At present, a visible-light-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) enzyme biosensor for glucose detection has been developed, utilizing reduced graphene oxide/titania (TiO2) nanotubes (RGO/TNTs) and glucose oxidase. The unique nanostructure enhances absorption and separates photo-generated electron-hole pairs, promoting PEC activity. The biosensor was highly sensitive, with a linear range and a low detection limit [4–6]. It was tested with human serum samples [6]. Furthermore, a proven mechanism for anodization and photoetching in the presence of UV light on the film has been proposed [7]. Therefore, it is necessary to select biomarkers with modified glucose biosensors for better sensitivity or specificity in the presence of UV light.

Utilizing a small volume of sample, the electrochemical biosensor has emerged as a compelling option for high-throughput analysis due to its enhanced sensitivity, speed, and selectivity in data collection [8–10]. Furthermore, despite technological advancements, the screen-printed electrode appears to be frequently utilized in electrochemical studies because of its portability, disposable nature, low cost, easy fabrication, quick response, and capacity to function as a point-of-care (POC) device. However, when screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) are employed without modification, their selectivity and sensitivity are principally limited by low current. Typically, a modifier that increases conductivity and quickens electron transfer needs to be added to the working electrode surface to increase SPCE efficiency.By increasing sensitivity, stability, and detection limits, these electrode alterations may enhance signal transduction at the electrode–electrolyte interchange [11,12].

The addition of composite nanomaterial-modified electrodes, such as titanium carbide (Ti3C2), known as MXenes, improves the electrochemical properties of the biosensor [13,14]. MXenes exhibit metallic conductivity, with some showing semiconductor behavior [15]. Surface functional groups are closely linked to electronic properties, and structural defects and mixed surface groups influence electrical conductivity [2]. Similar to TiO2 nanotubes, Ti3C2 can effectively increase the potential PEC performance by increasing carrier concentration, extending excitons' lifetime, decreasing electron-hole recombination, and boosting photogenerated carrier separation [16]. Our recent pioneer report involved MXene and graphene quantum dots (GQD). This composite was discovered to enhance electrochemical behavior, resulting in a greater peak current. Moreover, this enhancement in performance could promise more efficient energy conversion processes, such as energy storage devices.

To achieve a stable dispersion between GQD and MXene, which is crucial for the enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity toward glucose detection [17], poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) will function in this nanocomposite as both an efficient electrical conductor and a polymeric binder [18]. An earlier study by Bunte and Ruhe (2009), describes a photochemical method for creating enzyme-containing redox polymer networks, which are critical for enzymatic sensors. The process involves incorporating photo-reactive benzophenone groups into polymers, which are then deposited on glassy carbon electrodes and cross-linked with UV light. The study also determines the effect of redox polymer chemistry on electrode performance [15,19,20]. Therefore, for electrode modification, we suggested PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique was utilized to analyze the variable concentration of glucose, which was the study's analyte. Additionally, the proposed biosensor's repeatability, stability, and interference effects have been studied. The proposed biosensor was found to be sufficiently sensitive for the detection of glucose by UV light, with high selectivity and stability using simple techniques without the need for cross-linkers. Furthermore, the biosensor's ability toperformeffectively under UV light conditions is notable, as material sensitivity to UV light is critical for efficient energy conversion from sunlight. This research aims to explore the advancements in the field of UV glucose biosensors, including the latest research findings and technological innovations that contribute to the enhancement of UV light stability in glucose electrochemical biosensors. By understanding and evaluating these advancements, we can gain insights into the potential applications and implications for improving glucose monitoring in both clinical and non-clinical settings. The modified electrode that was selected to identify glucose in the presence of the enzyme is shown as a flow chart in Fig. S1.

Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA, provided glucose, glucose oxidase (GOx), and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-polystyrene-sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS, 666201). Graphene quantum dot (GNQD0101) was obtained from ACS Materials, and MXene (Ti3C2), was produced using the standard exfoliation method of MAXphase (Ti3AlC2) [23]. Sigma-Aldrich acetic acid and ammonia were mixed to make a 0.1 M ammonium acetate solution with a pH of 7. R&M Chemicals in Malaysia provided the potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6. Throughout the experiments, DI water was used.

All electrochemical detection was carried out using the mini PSTAT 910 (Metrohm Ltd. Switzerland) controlled by the computer. The FTIR spectroscopy (FT/IR-6100 TypeA, JASCO) was carried out to examine the functional group of composite materials. A scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL) was used to observe the surface morphologies of nanocomposites on SPCE. Screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE)with a diameter of 4 mm was purchased from Rapid Genesis Sdn Bhd (Malaysia) which consists of the working electrode (carbon), reference electrode (AgCl), and auxiliary electrode (carbon), respectively. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) system (JEOL, JEM-2010) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was employed to ascertain the dimensions and distribution of the nanomaterials.
Under different preparation conditions (CV and DPV), signal response curves were measured using SPCE modified with PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx.

This study used an electrochemical transducer (potentiostat) linked with a computer to facilitate the monitoring of electrochemical measurements. Fig. S2 provides an overview of the system. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were conducted using a 5 mM solution of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The CV scan range was set from −0.4 V to 0.6 V, with a scan rate of 30 mV/s. The DPV scan range was set to −0.1 V to 0.2 V, with a rate of 10 mV/s. The electrochemical measurement setup comprised a three-electrode system, comprising a carbon electrode (0.04 cm2) serving as the working electrode, a saturated silver electrode as the reference electrode, and carbon as the counter electrode.

Bare SPCEs were pre-treated by rinsing with ethanol and DI water and dried under nitrogen. A composite suspension of PEDOT:PSS, MXene, and graphene quantum dots (GQD) was prepared in water and sonicated for 30 min. 10 µL of this suspension was drop-cast onto the working area of SPCE and dried at room temperature. For enzyme immobilization, 10 µL of 100 U/mL GOx was drop-cast onto the modified SPCE and allowed to dry at 4 °C overnight. Control electrodes were prepared without GOx to evaluate the electrochemical response at each modification step.

Response surface methodology is an effective statistical technique for analyzing multivariate experiments using numerous quadratic regression equations. Response surface methodology (RSM) is the most common technology used in industrial research. As a result, using RSM to study the interplay of process parameters and determine optimal operating parameters may help improve work efficiency and model development. Because the process parameters of this experiment were limited to a restricted area, the Box-Behnken design (BBD) was able to simplify the experiment while preserving model accuracy. The experiments were designed with RSM based on BBD and Design-Expert software, version 13.0.1.0 (Stat-Ease, Inc., Minneapolis, USA). The impacts of three independent factors were investigated: pH, current density, and enzyme layers. The rationale for selecting these three criteria is as follows: The pH level is likely to affect the current structure of amoxicillin molecules. This is an important parameter in electrochemical systems, as current density influences the efficiency of hydroxyl radical production. Ultimately, the initial concentration of composites influenced the current density peak for glucose detection. When the electrolyte concentration was optimal, no significant impact on the current density was observed.BBD can provide a graphical relationship between parameters, as shown in Equation (3) [21]:
(1)
Y =
β 0
+
∑
j = 1
K
β j
x j
+
∑
j = 1
K
β
jj
x j 2
+
∑ i
∑ j K
β
ij
x i
x j
+
r i
i < j
where Y is the predicted response; Xi, Xj, and XiXj are the independent variables’ effects, square effects, and interaction effects in terms of coded factors, respectively; βi, βii, and βij are the polynomial coefficients; β0 is a random error

The formation of Ti integrated with PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was confirmed by comparing its structure, surface, and particle distribution with PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). In Fig. 1 A. The enzyme electrodeposition method produced a consistent distribution of spherical objects (Fig. 1A). These findings are consistent with previous studies [22,23]. In contrast, the electrodeposition of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx exhibits a distinct morphology, manifesting as darker spots and an average diameter of approximately 23 nm (n = 30), as illustrated in Fig. 1B. Fig. 1A also shows a top-view SEM image of Ti3C2, revealing its characteristic two-dimensional multilayered structure and restacking of the sheets, which is consistent with previous reports [24]. As can be seen, PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx has greater surface roughness, indicating that the addition of glucose enzymes increases the roughness due to the cavities formed on the surface. As a result, it has a larger surface area and, thus, a greater capacity to adsorb template molecules [25].

The electrodeposition of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD, as illustrated in Fig. 1, was subjected to thorough SEM and FESEM characterization to determine the morphology of the nanocomposite in detail.As illustrated in Fig. 1A, the top view of MXene images exhibits restacking of the sheet and two-dimensional multilayers, which is consistent with the findings of the previous report [26]. The GQD morphology was investigated using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), which revealed a quasi-spherical, homogeneously distributed, and monodisperse structure with an average diameter of less than 10 nm. Furthermore, the morphology and structure of the composite nanomaterial (PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD) were previously analyzed using SEM, which revealed a more aggregated, flaky, and rough surface. This is due to the high surface energy between the MXene and GQD nanoparticles, present in agglomeration [18].

To calculate the electroactive surface area (A) of an electrode, plot anodic currents (I) vs. scan rate (V1/2) and scan in the range of 25–––150 mV/s (Fig. 2 A-B). The Randles-Servick equation (Ipa = ¼ (2.69 × 105)n3/2AD1/2C*V1/2) was used to calculate the effective surface area of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx, which was 0.0748 cm2 compared to bare electrode 0.071 cm2. Ipa represents the oxidation peak current (A), n represents the electron transfer number, A represents the electroactive surface area (cm−2), D represents the diffusion coefficient of K4[Fe(CN)6] in the solution, C* represents the concentration of K4[Fe(CN)6], and V represents the scan rate (V s−1) [27]. This also indicates that the effective surface area for modified SPCE is high compared to bare SPCE suggesting that the increase in the electroactive surface was attributed mainly to PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx deposition.

Fig. 2A shows cyclic voltammograms for 25 to 150 mV/s scan rate effects in KCl of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx. The electrodes' characteristic changes in electrochemical response demonstrate that the catalytically active enzyme has been successfully integrated into the redox polymer matrix and is electrically communicating with it. The constant electron transfer from the enzyme to the polymer units causes the oxidation current to increase. This suggested that the increase in the electroactive surface was largely attributed to PEDOT:PSS with MXene [24]. This also indicates that the electron transfer process in the GQD redox reaction was a surface-controlled mechanism [28]. A linear calibration (Fig. 2B) with a coefficient regression of 0.999, respectively, showed that the process is diffusion-controlled.

To improve the sensitivity of the PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx modified electrode, the pH was optimized in the range of 4.5 to 8.0. As shown in Fig. 2C, current density increased until pH 7.0, then decreased dramatically at pH 8.0. This demonstrated that enzyme activity was greatest at pH 7.0 for this modified electrode [29]. Because an excess of either acid or alkali will deactivate an enzyme, pH 7 was determined to be the ideal setting for the biosensor's construction, taking into account the biological activity of the enzyme [6]. Then, before assessing the pH range, the enzyme (GOx) layers on the electrode surface were identified. This led to increased current density up to the third layer and a slight slowdown in the subsequent layer (Fig. 2D).

The demonstrated selectivity in Fig. 4
E suggests that the electrode could efficiently function as a glucose sensor even under UV light exposure. Meanwhile, the biosensors' stability and performance were evaluated by monitoring their responses to glucose standard solutions over 5 weeks. To demonstrate selectivity, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and creatinine were chosen as the interferences [30]. Each interference was used at high concentrations, as Fig. 2E demonstrates. After removing the interferences, the photocurrent remained unchanged, but the presence of target glucose alone or combined with these interferences significantly inhibited it. No significant response was observed for the addition of ascorbic acid, uric acid, and creatinine from the current density changes. These findings indicate that SPCE/PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD/GOx is highly selective for glucose 50 µM and has almost no interference with AA, UA, and creatinine, each at a concentration of 50 µM. The phenomenon demonstrated that the protocol has a significant potential for developing sensitive PEC enzymatic biosensors [31]. Because of that, this system demonstrated outstanding analytical performance, including ultra-high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, reduced background noise, and dual-modality operation. The selection of a photoelectrochemical (PEC) approach in this study is well-justified by the synergistic light-assisted charge separation observed within the PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD composite significantly enhances sensor performance.

The photoelectrochemical stability of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on SPCE was also evaluated. Fig. 3A-C shows the increase in current density performance for ambient, dark, and UV light conditions as the glucose concentration increases. As shown in Fig. 3D, the photocurrent response of a PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx current density changes slightly to 108 µA/cm2 at 500 µM glucose concentration under successive illumination, indicating the PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD possesses high photoelectrochemical stability as it is excited with a 395 nm laser [29].

Based on Fig. 3E, the linear regression equation for glucose concentration and photocurrent was shown, where Y = 0.0257x + 106.27 at glucose concentrations between 0 and 500 µM. Thus, at the same range of glucose concentrations, the sensitivity was 5.41 μAμM−1 cm−2, and the LOD was 44.60 μM. The electrode's adsorption of intermediates is primarily responsible for segmenting the test findings. Under UV light conditions for glucose detection, the linearity is good, with a greater slope due to the free pervasion of glucose molecules toward the electrode. The photoactive components (GQD and MXene) enabled UV-light-driven electron excitation, improving photocurrent and sensor sensitivity. This UV photoactivation mimics bio-solar systems and enhances the signal-to-noise ratio [4]. In the darkness, the interaction between the glucose molecules becomes more intense, resulting in a less linear curve with the lowest slope. As a result, sensitivities are found to be linear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RSM statistical analysis of optimization parameters

ce:section.ce:para:
The PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx was optimized utilizing the Central Composite Design, which has three levels and three factors. This technique verified the optimal conditions discovered during the studies. pH (A), scan rate (B), and enzyme layers (C) were investigated for their impact on the overall operational reaction. The response variable was current density (Y), and the link between the different parameters was determined using a full quadratic model. Equation (2) shows the estimated coefficients of the quadratic regression model using the encoded parameters.
(2)
Y = 136 . 0 4 + 0 . 662 0 ∗ A - 17 . 57 ∗ B + 5 0 . 62 ∗ C + 1 . 69 ∗ AB - 0 . 2381 ∗ AC + 0 . 2919 ∗ BC - 25 . 13 ∗
A
2
- 15 . 0 4 ∗
B
2
- 65 . 17 ∗
C
2
where A, B, and C correspond to the coded forms of pH, scan rate, and enzyme layers, respectively. The interaction terms are AB, AC, and BC, while A2, B2, and C2 are the squared terms associated with these independent variables.

The design was implemented, and 20 runs were subsequently analyzed in a randomized sequence, with each run comprising five replications. Fig. 4 presents an illustration of diagnostic diagrams for the comparison of data. Fig. 4A illustrates the presence of normally distributed residuals, as indicated by the alignment of all values along a straight line. Nevertheless, considerable scatter is evident, which is to be anticipated for data that follows a normal distribution. This illustrates that Equation (1) is an accurate model for describing the experimental results. Furthermore, the scatter plot of residuals versus the number of runs of the experiment (Fig. 4B) demonstrates that no data point is situated outside the prescribed range. This finding suggests that no unobserved variables had an impact on the outcome of the experiment. The existence of specific trends, on the other hand, shows that a time-associated variable is operating in the background. Blocking and randomization trials can serve as efficient controls to limit the impact of these patterns and ensure correct analysis.

Furthermore, Central Composite Design can be utilized to discover the best power transformation for the response dataset. This adjustment is required when the residuals are proportionate to the expected values. The lowest point on the Central Composite Design represents the optimal lambda, which lowers the model's residual sum of squares. The suggested lambda value is 7.5. Thus, using the central composite design depicted in Fig. 4C, it is clear that the study's current lambda value within the 95 % confidence interval is one. The number displayed above indicates that no data modifications were conducted. In contrast, Fig. 4D compares the experimental and predicted data for current density. The results demonstrated a strong correlation between the anticipated values and the actual values determined by the experimental data. As a result, the scattered dots in Fig. 4 stayed very close to the straight line, showing a reasonable match between expected and real data.

Table S1 highlights the results of an ANOVA test to determine the relevance and application of the quadratic approach for PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx. Table S1 depicts the impact of significant individual phrases and how they interact with the chosen responses. This model's degree of freedom (F-value) was 2754.92, and the p-values were less than 0.0001, indicating its significance. Based on the F-value and p-value (both less than 0.05), this model demonstrated statistical significance for current density.

In the study by Hu et al.[32], the p-value is defined as the probability of an incorrect result and is used to determine the significance of each regression result. Referring to the major model, terms A, B, and C have a significant influence on the current density peak, and there is a correlation between the three principal components (AB, AC, and BC). Meanwhile, A2, B2, and C2 are notable quadratic components of the response. Accordingly, the p-values presented in Table S1 were all less than 0.05, substantiating the model's significance.

According to Ban et al.[33], a minor lack of fit is to be preferred, since a substantial lack of fit may suggest unidentified contributions to the regressor-response connection for PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx. In this study, the coefficient of variation (CV) was 13.78 %, indicating good precision and consistency in the experimental results. Moreover, the coefficient of determination (R2) was 0.9642, demonstrating that the model effectively accounted for the majority of the variability in the response. The adjusted R2 value of 0.9183 further supports the model’s reliability, confirming that it provides a robust and accurate approximation of the reaction behavior within the studied range. Consistent with these findings, the ANOVA results summarized in Table S1 show that the scan rate had the most significant influence on the response (F-value = 43.54), followed by the pH value (F-value = 20.98), and the number of enzyme layers (F-value = 6.45), highlighting their respective contributions to the system’s performance.

RSM was implemented to optimize the current conductivity process utilizing PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx and the Central Composite Design (CCD) was employed to produce the experimental design matrix. The 3D response plot depicts the connection between two input variables and the response variable. It is an effective tool for visualizing the interaction of variables and determining the best circumstances for a maximal reaction. Furthermore, the contour plot is an effective tool for visualizing the response surface. It is a 2D graphic that demonstrates the connection between two input variables and the response variable by charting the same response surface at different response levels [33].

Fig. 5 A depicts a 3D surface plot and a 2D contour plot of the interaction effect of pH and scan rate on the current density peak for the PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx glucose detection system. The specified response time was set to one hour. Five duplicates of the designed center point in the center of the designed space gave satisfactory predictability, as indicated by the red point in the center of the RSM 3D surface plot and 2D contour plot [33]. As the pH and scan rate increased (from 4.5 to 8.0 and 25 to 150 mV/s, respectively), the current density increased until the maximum peak was attained. This change is probably due to PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx being active and thus improving the interaction between PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD and the enzyme (GOx). The current density showed a small decrease at high pH values (8.0) and scan rates (100 mV/s) due to the conductivity of the current system. Once the reaction rates were optimal, the equilibrium began to shift toward a reverse reaction.

While the scan rate was kept constant at 150 mV/s, Fig. 5B shows the effect of enzyme layers and pH on the current density peak. Notably, the current density peak increased as enzyme layers and scan rate increased until the maximum peak was reached; however, increasing enzyme layers beyond the third layer decreased the current density due to reversible electron transfer kinetics [34] whereas continuously increasing scan rate increased the current density. Furthermore, Fig. 5C shows that the enzyme layers and scan rate influenced current density, whereas the pH level remained constant at 7.0. Using the third enzyme layer, the current density reached over 450 µA/cm2 at a scan rate of 150 mV/s. Thus, the elliptical contour plots in Fig. 5 highlight the relevance and efficiency of the link between the three independent variables in the current density peak.

Most importantly, the limit of glucose detection using PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx was obtained at 44.60 μM. through an experiment with Y = 0.0257x + 106.27 (R2 = 0.965), which was comparable with previous reports given in Table 1 .

An efficient photocurrent density for glucose detection was evaluated based on PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on the SPCE sensing. The specific interactions of UV with composites resulted in increasing incorporation of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on the SPCE surface, which significantly increased the surface area, leading toimproved selectivity, sensitivity, and electron transfer rates with linear relationships between the oxidation current peak and [glucose] in the range of 0 to 500 µM and a low LOD of 44.60 μM. Compared to other potential interferents, the responses of the current peak to hydrogen were rapid, selective, and sensitive. The experimental results demonstrated that this PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on SPCE had high reliability towards UV light practicability for glucose determination. This study's findings could have implications for energy-related applications, particularly in improving energy conversion efficiency and monitoring processes critical to energy systems. Next, the optimization study of PEDOT:PSS/MXene/GQD-GOx on SPCE using RSM also proved that the maximum current density was obtained at a pH value of 7.0 at the third layer of an enzyme using a scan rate of 150 mV/s. The findings from the ANOVA data revealed that the current density was the most significant variable in the RSM study, with an F-value of 22.44.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and UNITEN supported the work through the BOLD Refresh Postdoctoral Fellowships under the project code J510050002-IC-6 BOLDREFRESH2025-Centre of Excellence.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An enzyme-free Ti3C2/Ni/Sm-LDH-based screen-printed-electrode for real-time sweat detection of glucose
